docs.text: Founding phase · Pilot community forming Journal of the Open Research Network Research is connected. Publishing should be too. JORN is a community-governed, Diamond Open Access overlay journal. We connect manuscripts, data, software, protocols, and transparent peer review into one trusted scholarly record. Join the founding pilot → Explore our model ↗ CONNECTED RECORD JORN · 0001 JOURNAL CERTIFIED RESEARCH ARTICLE One record for the complete research story Authors · ORCID · CRediT M Manuscript v3 D Dataset DOI C Software v1.2 R Open reviews 2 Transparent history CC BY 4.0 £0 Author fees 100% Open access Open Peer review Connected Research objects JORN UPDATE We are building the founding editorial community. Researchers, reviewers, and partner organizations are invited to help shape the pilot. Take part → A journal for open research Keep the research where it belongs. Connect it where it matters. Research does not live in a single PDF. It spans repositories, codebases, datasets, protocols, and conversations. JORN provides the editorial layer that reviews, connects, and certifies these distributed objects—without rebuilding the infrastructure that already serves researchers well. Why JORN → 01 Distributed by design Manuscripts, data, and software remain in trusted repositories built to host and preserve them. 02 Certified with care Editors and independent reviewers assess the research and identify the exact version accepted. 03 Transparent by default Versions, reviews, responses, decisions, and corrections form a visible editorial history. JORN certified record M Manuscript arXiv · bioRxiv · Zenodo D Data DataCite DOI C Software Repository + archive R Peer review Reports + responses O Contributors ORCID + CRediT One scholarly record Everything needed to understand, verify, and build on the work. Every JORN publication will be a persistent certification record: the authoritative map between an accepted manuscript, the evidence behind it, and the people and process that evaluated it. Exact reviewed and accepted versions Linked data, software, protocols, and preregistrations Editorial decisions, reviews, and author responses Machine-readable metadata and persistent identifiers See the publishing model → How publishing works Rigorous review. A lighter footprint. Authors begin by sharing their work in an appropriate open repository. JORN focuses on the work a journal uniquely must do: editorial assessment, peer review, certification, and stewardship. Full editorial process → 1 Deposit Share the manuscript and research objects in trusted open repositories. 2 Connect Provide persistent links for data, software, protocols, identities, and funding. 3 Review Editors and independent peers assess validity, transparency, and reproducibility. 4 Certify JORN identifies the accepted version and publishes the complete editorial record. Initial scope Research that is open in practice, not only in access. JORN’s founding scope centers work where openness, connected evidence, and reproducibility are essential to the contribution. Explore aims & scope → Research software Data-intensive research Reproducibility reports Replication studies Open methods Research infrastructure Registered reports Community-led research Our publishing principles Open for readers. Fair for authors. Accountable to the community. No APCs Diamond Open Access No subscriptions, paywalls, or mandatory publication fees. Author-owned Rights retained Authors keep copyright; CC BY 4.0 is the preferred article license. Community-led Transparent governance Roles, decisions, funding, partnerships, and policy changes are public. Interoperable Open infrastructure Open-source tools, public metadata, persistent identifiers, and exportable records. Help shape JORN A journal built with its community from the start. A Authors Propose work for the pilot collection → R Reviewers & editors Join the founding editorial network → P Partners Connect infrastructure and communities → A more connected scholarly record Open research, reviewed by an open network. Discover JORN →
docs.title: Home

docs.text: Why JORN? ¶ Research is increasingly distributed. A manuscript may be on arXiv, data in Zenodo, software on GitHub and Software Heritage, a protocol in a specialist repository, and contributor identities in ORCID. Yet the formal publication often reduces that connected work to a PDF and a supplement link. JORN is designed around a different premise: the journal should be the trusted editorial and certification layer, not the owner of every research object. Open research, reviewed by an open network. JORN’s founding vision What the journal will do ¶ JORN will be directly accountable for the scholarly functions that cannot safely be delegated: Select & review ¶ Define scope, appoint qualified editors, coordinate independent review, and make decisions. Connect & certify ¶ Identify exact versions, link research objects, and publish authoritative metadata. Protect integrity ¶ Manage conflicts, ethics, corrections, retractions, complaints, and appeals. Steward the record ¶ Maintain discoverability, preservation planning, continuity, and public accountability. Our place in the network ¶ Open Research Network is the wider community and open-research initiative. JORN is its proposed peer-review, publication, and scholarly-certification program. Open Science Labs supports the initiative and its development. The relationship is intended to bring researchers, independent scholars, open-source contributors, research software engineers, data communities, and reproducibility initiatives into a shared publication program. Current status ¶ JORN is in a concept and founding phase. Its name, exact scope, governance, platform, legal responsibilities, and editorial policies require formal due diligence and community agreement before launch. This website presents the direction of travel transparently; it does not claim that the journal is already accepting or certifying research.

docs.title: Current status

docs.text: Proposed governance structure ¶ STRATEGY ### Steering Council Sets mission and long-term strategy, oversees sustainability and partnerships, appoints senior editors, and approves major policy changes. EDITORIAL ### Editorial Board Maintains editorial standards and scope, selects reviewers, makes publication decisions, and develops specialist sections. COMMUNITY ### Community Advisory Group Brings perspectives from open science, software and data communities, universities, independent researchers, and early-career scholars. INTEGRITY ### Research Integrity Group Advises on misconduct, retractions, expressions of concern, undisclosed conflicts, manipulation, and complex ethical questions. INFRASTRUCTURE ### Technical & Metadata Group Oversees the platform, repository connections, DOI deposits, metadata quality, preservation, automation, and accessibility. Founding editorial team ¶ A credible starting team is expected to include a coordinating editor or editor-in-chief, a managing editor, five to eight editorial board members, specialist section editors, and advisers for research integrity, reproducibility, software, data, and statistics. Members should represent more than one institution and publish their roles, affiliations, relevant interests, and terms of service. Editorial safeguards ¶ Decisions are made by editors with relevant expertise and no disqualifying conflict. Work submitted by an editor or board member receives separate, independent handling. Sponsors and partners cannot influence acceptance, rejection, or review reports. Complaints and appeals follow a documented process. Funding, sponsorship, partnerships, policy changes, and annual reports are public. Governance documents and decision-making procedures remain accessible and versioned. Formation process ¶ The structure shown here is a proposal, not a claim that every body has already been appointed. Founding members will refine mandates, appointment processes, terms, diversity goals, conflict procedures, and routes for community input before the journal begins formal review.

docs.title: Formation process

docs.text: An overlay journal ¶ A conventional journal often combines submission, file storage, peer review, production, metadata, and preservation in one system. JORN’s proposed overlay model separates these responsibilities so that each service can focus on what it does best. Authors deposit research objects in established open services. JORN then provides editorial selection, peer review, integrity checks, certification, publication metadata, and continuing stewardship. A distributed architecture ¶ Research component Possible host JORN’s role Manuscript or preprint arXiv, bioRxiv, medRxiv, Zenodo, or another trusted repository Review and certify an exact version Data Zenodo, Figshare, OSF, institutional or disciplinary repositories Assess availability, documentation, licensing, and links Software GitHub, GitLab, Codeberg, archived releases, Software Heritage Review code, tests, documentation, license, and reproducibility Protocol or preregistration OSF, protocols.io, Zenodo, specialist services Connect the study plan and methods to the reported work Researcher identity ORCID Reliably identify authors, editors, and reviewers Contributor roles CRediT Describe individual contributions Journal record JORN Publish the certification, review history, metadata, and status Two identifiers, two purposes ¶ The repository identifier describes the deposited research object and its version history. A separate JORN DOI will identify the journal-certified publication. Metadata will connect the two, allowing readers to distinguish the preprint from the accepted work without erasing the research chronology. What JORN cannot outsource ¶ The journal may distribute hosting and technical work, but it remains accountable for its identity, scope, governance, editorial independence, reviewer selection, final decisions, conflicts, integrity investigations, corrections, retractions, metadata, and continuity. Platform direction ¶ The first option to evaluate is Episciences, which is designed for repository-based overlay journals. Open Journal Systems or a lightweight open workflow may be considered if they better meet JORN’s needs. A fully custom platform is not the preferred starting point because it would add avoidable security, privacy, maintenance, and preservation responsibilities. Preservation and portability ¶ Distributed publishing does not eliminate preservation duties. JORN will need exportable metadata, off-site backups, archived editorial records, stable identifiers, documented migration procedures, domain continuity, and a succession plan. It should avoid dependence on any single commercial platform.
docs.title: Publishing model

docs.text: Policy framework in development. The summaries below state the intended direction and are not yet final author guidance. Access, rights, and fees ¶ JORN is intended to be Diamond Open Access: no subscriptions, paywalls, article-processing charges, or mandatory publication fees. Authors retain copyright. CC BY 4.0 is the preferred default license for article content; data and software should use suitable open licenses or dedications. Peer review and editorial decisions ¶ Research articles will normally receive at least two independent reviews. Reviewer expertise, methodological fit, conflicts, institutional independence, and diversity of perspective will inform selection. Final decisions belong to an unconflicted editor and will not be controlled by sponsors or partners. Research integrity ¶ The final framework will address plagiarism, fabrication and falsification, image or data manipulation, authorship disputes, undisclosed conflicts, corrections, expressions of concern, retractions, and withdrawal. JORN will remain responsible for the integrity of its certification even when files are hosted elsewhere. Evidence, data, and software ¶ Authors should make supporting research objects available in appropriate repositories with persistent identifiers, licenses, documentation, and exact versions. Legitimate restrictions involving privacy, consent, security, or community governance must be explained and handled through an approved access route—not ignored. Policies required for launch ¶ Aims and scope Author guidance Peer review Editorial decisions Publication ethics Authorship and CRediT roles Conflicts of interest Data and software Reproducibility Preregistration AI use Human-participant ethics Animal research Corrections and retractions Withdrawal Appeals and complaints Copyright and licensing Privacy and data protection Fees Reviewer and editor conduct Sponsorship and advertising Accessibility ¶ The journal aims to meet WCAG 2.2 AA as its public baseline. Pages should support keyboard navigation, visible focus, semantic structure, sufficient contrast, responsive layouts, meaningful alternative text, and reduced-motion preferences. Accessibility issues will have a clear reporting route before launch. Versioning and accountability ¶ Final policies will display an effective date, revision history, and responsible governance body. Material changes will be announced and preserved so that authors and reviewers can determine which rules applied to a particular editorial record.

docs.text: The proposed workflow ¶ 01 Deposit The author deposits a licensed, versioned manuscript in an appropriate open repository. Data, software, protocols, and other objects are deposited in services suited to preserving them. 02 Connect The submission identifies every relevant research object alongside ORCID records, CRediT roles, ethics, funding, prior versions, and competing interests. 03 Editorial screening An editor assesses scope, plausibility, integrity, repository eligibility, licensing, ethics, evidence availability, and the need for specialist review. 04 Independent review Qualified reviewers evaluate scientific validity, methods, reporting, reproducibility, data, and software. Research articles normally receive at least two reviews. 05 Revision Authors deposit a new version and respond to reviews. JORN records what changed, further comments, and the editor’s assessment. 06 Acceptance & certification JORN identifies the exact accepted version, publishes the decision and review record, registers publication metadata, and links the complete research record. 07 Continuing stewardship The journal maintains corrections, expressions of concern, retractions, metadata updates, appeals, and the integrity of its certification. Open peer review ¶ JORN is working toward a review model that makes the reasoning behind certification visible while protecting participants where openness could introduce genuine risk. The founding board will define when reviews are signed, anonymous, public during evaluation, or published after acceptance. Whatever model applies, authors and reviewers will know the terms before participating. Conflicts will be documented and editorial decisions will be made by an unconflicted editor. What reviewers assess ¶ Scientific and methodological validity Statistical analysis and reporting Reproducibility and documentation Data availability and provenance Software quality, testing, and licensing Ethics and responsible research practice Alignment with protocols or preregistration Clarity, accessibility, and contribution Editorial independence ¶ Sponsors, infrastructure providers, partner communities, and the supporting organization will not determine editorial outcomes. Submissions from editors or board members will be handled independently under a published conflict-of-interest procedure. The journal will publish routes for appeals and complaints before formal submissions open.
docs.title: Editorial process

docs.text: JORN is working toward a review model that makes the reasoning behind certification visible while protecting participants where openness could introduce genuine risk. The founding board will define when reviews are signed, anonymous, public during evaluation, or published after acceptance. Whatever model applies, authors and reviewers will know the terms before participating. Conflicts will be documented and editorial decisions will be made by an unconflicted editor.

docs.title: Editorial independence

docs.text: The relationship ¶ The Open Research Network is the broader community and collaboration initiative. JORN is its proposed peer-review and scholarly-certification program. Open Science Labs supports the development of both. This structure is intended to welcome contributors who often sit across institutional boundaries: independent researchers, open-source maintainers, research software engineers, data scientists, librarians, reproducibility initiatives, and disciplinary communities. Ways to participate ¶ 01 Authors Propose open and reproducible work for the founding pilot and help us test the author journey. Pilot information → 02 Reviewers & editors Contribute subject, methods, software, data, statistics, or integrity expertise to the founding network. Review principles → 03 Community partners Connect disciplinary groups, repositories, institutions, libraries, and open-infrastructure projects. Publishing model → 04 Advisers Help shape governance, accessibility, privacy, metadata, ethics, preservation, and sustainable operations. Governance proposal → Partner principles ¶ Partnership does not confer editorial privilege. Any formal relationship should be transparent, mission-aligned, and governed by written safeguards for editorial independence. JORN should prefer partners that support open standards, portable data, persistent identifiers, and community accountability. Potential connections may include open repositories, scholarly identifier services, preservation networks, universities, libraries, research-software communities, and independent research organizations. Names will only be presented as partners after a relationship is confirmed. Founding roadmap ¶ 01 Feasibility Mission, scope, publisher, platform, legal needs, and founding group 02 Policy & governance Editors, peer review, integrity, conflicts, data, software, and ethics 03 Technical prototype Submission, metadata, repositories, DOI workflow, review, and preservation 04 Pilot collection Five to ten independently reviewed submissions and workflow evaluation 05 Public launch Open submissions, first collection, identifiers, discovery, and reporting Follow the work ¶ JORN’s development will take place openly wherever privacy and editorial integrity permit. Public project links and participation forms will be added as the founding governance is confirmed. Build the network with us ¶ Share the project with a community that cares about open, rigorous, and reproducible research—or support the infrastructure and coordination required to launch it responsibly. Support JORN View the project on GitHub

docs.text: FOUNDING PHASE The pilot collection is in development ¶ JORN is establishing its editorial board, policies, and technical workflow before opening formal submissions. We will never imply that work has been peer reviewed or certified until the complete process is in place. Propose work for the pilot Follow our progress More than an article page ¶ A JORN publication will identify exactly what was evaluated, how it changed, and which supporting objects belong to it. Every record is designed for both human readers and machine discovery. RESEARCH ARTICLE CERTIFIED JORN · YEAR · DOI Article title and complete citation Authors · affiliations · ORCID records · CRediT roles Manuscript Reviewed and accepted versions Evidence Data, methods, and protocols Software Source and archived release Review history Reports, responses, and decision Integrity record Ethics, funding, and interests Living status Corrections and later updates Planned publication types ¶ Research articles ¶ Original research with openly available evidence and a clearly traceable research workflow. Software & data papers ¶ Scholarly contributions centered on reusable research software, datasets, and infrastructure. Reproducibility reports ¶ Replications, registered reports, negative results, and rigorous evaluations of prior findings. Continuous publication ¶ Accepted work will be published as soon as its record is complete, then grouped into annual volumes. A journal DOI will identify the certified publication while repository identifiers continue to identify the underlying preprint, data, and software. This separation keeps the history of the research visible and prevents a preprint from being confused with a journal-certified version.

docs.title: Continuous publication

docs.text: Our aim ¶ The Journal of the Open Research Network aims to make open research easier to evaluate, trust, reuse, and extend. It will provide scholarly certification for work already deposited in open repositories and connect the parts of research that conventional article pages often leave scattered. JORN will judge work on methodological quality, clarity, integrity, reproducibility, and the value of its contribution—not on predicted popularity or narrow claims of novelty. Founding subject areas ¶ 01 Reproducible computational research Analyses with accessible code, environments, and evidence. 02 Research software Software with a clear scholarly contribution, documentation, tests, and an archived release. 03 Data-intensive research Research in which data quality, provenance, and reuse are central. 04 Methods and protocols Open methods that enable reliable and transferable research practice. 05 Replication and meta-research Studies that test, synthesize, or improve how knowledge is produced. 06 Open research infrastructure Tools, standards, and community systems supporting open scholarship. Publication types ¶ The initial portfolio may include research articles, software papers, dataset papers, reproducibility reports, replication studies, methods papers, registered reports, research notes, and open-infrastructure reports. The founding editorial board will establish precise criteria before submissions open. What every submission should demonstrate ¶ A clear research question, contribution, or reuse case Appropriate and transparently reported methods Public access to the manuscript and all shareable supporting objects Documentation sufficient for informed evaluation and reuse Persistent identifiers and version information Ethical and legal handling of people, animals, communities, and sensitive data Complete authorship, funding, and competing-interest disclosures Out of scope ¶ JORN will not certify work simply because it is publicly available. Incomplete submissions, unverifiable claims, inappropriate repository deposits, missing ethical approval, undisclosed conflicts, or inaccessible central evidence may be declined during screening. During the pilot, the journal may also decline sound work when the editorial network cannot provide the specialist expertise required for responsible review.
docs.title: Aims & scope

JORN will judge work on methodological quality, clarity, integrity, reproducibility, and the value of its contribution—not on predicted popularity or narrow claims of novelty.

Incomplete submissions, unverifiable claims, inappropriate repository deposits, missing ethical approval, undisclosed conflicts, or inaccessible central evidence may be declined during screening. During the pilot, the journal may also decline sound work when the editorial network cannot provide the specialist expertise required for responsible review.
docs.title: Out of scope

docs.text: Submissions are not yet formally open. We are inviting expressions of interest for a small, independently reviewed founding pilot. Is JORN right for your work? ¶ The initial collection will prioritize open, reproducible, computational, software, data, methods, replication, and community-led research. Every submission must make its central research objects openly accessible and persistently identifiable. 01 ### Public manuscript A versioned manuscript in an approved open repository. 02 ### Connected evidence Persistent links to relevant data, software, protocols, and preregistration. 03 ### Open licensing Clear licenses that permit access and appropriate reuse. 04 ### Complete disclosures Authorship, contributions, ethics, funding, and competing interests. Prepare an expression of interest ¶ Founding-pilot proposals should be concise. Please be ready to provide: A title and structured abstract The manuscript repository and exact version identifier ORCID identifiers and CRediT contributor roles Links to data, software, protocols, and preregistrations Ethics approval or an explanation of why it is not applicable Funding and conflict-of-interest statements A short explanation of the work’s fit with JORN’s founding scope Sensitive and medical research ¶ Health-related work, human-participant research, and sensitive data require specialist editorial screening. Sensitive data must never be placed in a public issue or an inappropriate repository. Formal guidance will be published before submissions open. What happens after submission? ¶ An editor first checks fit, completeness, integrity, repository eligibility, ethics, licensing, and the availability of supporting objects. Research articles will normally receive at least two independent reviews. The handling editor remains responsible for the final decision. Read the editorial process Join the founding pilot ¶ We are currently seeking a small and diverse group of potential authors to test the complete workflow. An expression of interest does not guarantee review or acceptance; all pilot submissions will face the same independent standards as later submissions. Register your interest ¶ The public intake form and contact address will be published after the founding editorial team and privacy process are confirmed. In the meantime, follow the Open Research Network’s project channels for launch updates. Explore ways to participate
docs.title: Overview

docs.text: The initial collection will prioritize open, reproducible, computational, software, data, methods, replication, and community-led research. Every submission must make its central research objects openly accessible and persistently identifiable. 01 ### Public manuscript A versioned manuscript in an approved open repository. 02 ### Connected evidence Persistent links to relevant data, software, protocols, and preregistration. 03 ### Open licensing Clear licenses that permit access and appropriate reuse. 04 ### Complete disclosures Authorship, contributions, ethics, funding, and competing interests.
docs.title: Is JORN right for your work?

docs.text: Founding-pilot proposals should be concise. Please be ready to provide: A title and structured abstract The manuscript repository and exact version identifier ORCID identifiers and CRediT contributor roles Links to data, software, protocols, and preregistrations Ethics approval or an explanation of why it is not applicable Funding and conflict-of-interest statements A short explanation of the work’s fit with JORN’s founding scope
docs.title: Prepare an expression of interest

docs.text: Health-related work, human-participant research, and sensitive data require specialist editorial screening. Sensitive data must never be placed in a public issue or an inappropriate repository. Formal guidance will be published before submissions open.
docs.title: Sensitive and medical research

docs.text: An editor first checks fit, completeness, integrity, repository eligibility, ethics, licensing, and the availability of supporting objects. Research articles will normally receive at least two independent reviews. The handling editor remains responsible for the final decision. Read the editorial process
docs.title: What happens after submission?

docs.text: We are currently seeking a small and diverse group of potential authors to test the complete workflow. An expression of interest does not guarantee review or acceptance; all pilot submissions will face the same independent standards as later submissions.
docs.title: Join the founding pilot

docs.text: The public intake form and contact address will be published after the founding editorial team and privacy process are confirmed. In the meantime, follow the Open Research Network’s project channels for launch updates. Explore ways to participate
docs.title: Register your interest

docs.text: What support makes possible ¶ JORN is being designed to avoid unnecessary infrastructure while investing in the scholarly work that cannot be automated away. Editorial coordination 01 Managing submissions, reviewers, decisions, policies, and the public record. Open infrastructure 02 Platform operation, repository connections, automation, security, and maintenance. Metadata & preservation 03 Identifiers, structured records, indexing, backups, archives, and continuity planning. Equity & accessibility 04 Accessible production, inclusive participation, and support for a global community. Ways to contribute ¶ Potential support may include grants, philanthropic funding, institutional and library partnerships, fiscal sponsorship, open-infrastructure programs, community donations, and carefully defined in-kind contributions. The journal will publish funding sources, sponsorship arrangements, annual financial summaries, and conflict-of-interest protections. Support will never buy authorship, favorable review, governance control, or influence over an editorial decision. Funding safeguards ¶ Editorial decisions remain institutionally and financially independent. Sponsorship terms and material in-kind support are publicly disclosed. No supporter receives privileged submission or review treatment. Restricted funding is accepted only when compatible with the mission. Financial reporting is understandable and available to the community. Sustainability plans avoid dependence on one funder or platform. Support route in preparation ¶ A formal donation and partnership route will be added after the publisher, fiscal responsibilities, and reporting process are confirmed. This protects both contributors and the journal during the founding phase. Contribute expertise ¶ Financial support is only one form of participation. JORN also needs editorial, research-integrity, technical, metadata, accessibility, preservation, legal, privacy, and community-building expertise. Explore the network
